In the middle of a cosmic showdown between the God of Israel and the false gods of Egypt, God does something that seems almost intimate. He doesn't simply declare that the firstborn will die at midnight. Instead, He gives His people a ritual to perform, a meal to eat, a sign to mark. They must select an unblemished lamb on the tenth day and keep it until the fourteenth—four days of living with the very creature that will die in their place. They must slaughter it, roast it, and paint their doorframes with its blood. They must eat it fully dressed for travel, ready to move, waiting for the moment when death will pass through the land. This is not abstract theology. This is visceral, physical, demanding obedience. God is teaching His people that deliverance requires a death—and that death must be witnessed, participated in, remembered. The blood on the doorframe is not a lucky charm or a magical symbol. It is a sign that God Himself will see and recognize. It declares: *I trust that God will do what He says.* It is the visible confession that life comes through substitution, that someone must die so that others might live.

What strikes with particular force is the completeness of God's judgment. At midnight, the destroyer goes through Egypt, and there is no discrimination. Pharaoh's firstborn son, the prisoner in the dungeon, the livestock in the fields—all of it falls under the same sentence. Egypt's gods are powerless to stop it. The wailing in the darkness is real. Families are shattered. And yet among the Israelites, in every house marked by blood, there is salvation. This is not because they are more righteous or worthy. They have been slaves in Egypt. They have no claim on God's favor except the covenant He made with Abraham. But God keeps His word. He distinguishes between those who trust His promise and those who reject it. The blood separates the saved from the destroyed, and there is no middle ground.

The chapter does not let us stay in the moment of deliverance. It pushes us forward, commanding that this night be remembered "as a permanent statute for the generations to come." The unleavened bread, the bitter herbs, the festive meal eaten in haste—all of it is meant to be repeated, retold, explained to children who will ask, "What does this service mean?" God is insisting that His people never forget what it cost to be free. Every year, the ritual becomes the memory; the memory becomes worship. The Israelites leave Egypt carrying silver and gold, goods plundered from their oppressors, but the real treasure is this: the knowledge that God is faithful, that He sees the blood, that He passes over those who trust Him. For Christians, this chapter stands as the shadow that Christ casts backward through history. Jesus is the Lamb without blemish. His blood, shed once for all, is the sign that God sees and recognizes. We do not need to repeat a ritual year after year because He died once, and the work is finished. But we do remember. Every time we approach God in prayer, every time we gather to worship, we return to this truth: deliverance has come through a death we did not deserve, marked by a blood we did not shed, offered by a God who keeps His word even when it costs everything.

The final image is of a people in motion, hundreds of thousands of them, leaving behind centuries of bondage in a single night. They do not know what lies ahead. They cannot see the Red Sea or the wilderness or the forty years to come. They only know that God has made a way out, that He has kept His promise, and that the only appropriate response is to move forward in obedience. That is what faith looks like in the darkness—not certainty about tomorrow, but willingness to walk forward because you have seen the God who keeps His word.
